ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for hot plate ginger onion chicken; closest parent 'chicken dish' (FOODON_03302757) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for hot plate ginger onion chicken. 'Chicken meat, cooked' (A00LC) used as closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 'Chicken dish (food)' is the closest parent; no specific code for this preparation exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hot plate serving of ginger onion chicken is approximately 300 g, typically comprising bone-in or boneless chicken pieces with ginger, spring onion, and sauce, served sizzling on a cast-iron plate.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, stir-fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05062); adapted with ginger-onion sauce composition from USDA Foundation Foods reference ingredients
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact match for hot plate ginger onion chicken exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy stir-fried chicken with skin (NDB 05062) as the primary protein-fat-energy anchor, with carbohydrate and sodium adjusted upward to account for soy sauce, oyster sauce, and cornstarch thickening typical of this dish. Estimated per-100g values reflect a mixed dish of approximately 65% chicken with skin and 35% sauce/aromatics by weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on stir-fried chicken with mixed sauce (low GI protein-dominant dish with moderate starch thickener)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hot plate ginger onion chicken. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous stir-fried chicken dishes with soy-based sauce and cornstarch thickener. Protein-dominant dishes with small amounts of cornstarch and soy sauce consistently fall in the low GI range (GI 30–45) per Atkinson et al. 2021 and University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar Asian chicken preparations. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 5.2 × 3.0) / 100 = 5.5 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~520 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1560 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a single 300 g serving provides approximately 1560 mg sodium, representing 68% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content (~5.2 g per 100 g) due to protein dominance and minimal starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot plate ginger onion chicken has a low glycemic index and low glycemic load per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without additional high-GI rice or noodles in excess.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.5 g per 100 g) and high sodium from sauce base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides moderate saturated fat and high sodium per serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose skinless chicken preparation and request reduced soy sauce to lower saturated fat and sodium intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Plate Ginger Onion Chicken is a Filipino-style sizzling chicken dish served on a preheated cast-iron plate that keeps the food hot throughout the meal. The dish consists of chicken pieces — typically bone-in thighs or drumsticks, or boneless breast — stir-fried with fresh ginger slices, spring onions, and onion wedges in a savory sauce made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and a cornstarch slurry for thickening. The hot plate presentation creates a dramatic sizzling effect when the sauce hits the heated surface, enhancing aroma and keeping the dish at serving temperature. The flavor profile is savory, slightly sweet, and aromatic, with the pungency of ginger balanced by the umami depth of soy and oyster sauce. Texturally, the chicken is tender and moist with a lightly glazed exterior. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found in Filipino cafes and casual dining restaurants, often served as a main course with steamed white rice. Nutritionally, it is a protein-rich dish (~16.8 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat and low carbohydrate content, giving it a low glycemic index of approximately 35. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) from the soy and oyster sauce base, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for most non-vegetarian dietary patterns.
common_user_queries: What is hot plate ginger onion chicken?
How many calories in hot plate ginger onion chicken?

confidence_reason: No exact match for hot plate ginger onion chicken was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy stir-fried chicken with skin as the primary anchor, with sauce-component adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is systematic and the analogous source is from an approved GI database.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for stir-fried chicken and is not measurable from the sauce components without specific laboratory analysis. 2. Nutrient values represent a per-100g estimate for the mixed dish (approximately 65% chicken with skin, 35% ginger-onion sauce) and may vary by restaurant preparation, sauce quantity, and whether skin-on or skinless chicken is used. 3. Halal status is marked false as Lucky Cafe - North Canal Road is a Filipino establishment and halal certification cannot be confirmed without direct verification; consumers requiring halal-certified food should verify with the restaurant. 4. The dish is served on a sizzling cast-iron plate which is a presentation method and does not materially alter the nutritional composition. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 6. Sodium content is a significant clinical concern; the per-serving estimate of approximately 1560 mg represents a substantial proportion of daily recommended intake.
